column2: What are the two primary functions NLP is divided into?
column3: Natural Language Understanding (NLU), which structures unstructured text, and Natural Language Generation (NLG), which produces language from data.

column2: What is Semantic Analysis (in NLP)?
column3: The specific process of drawing meaning by analyzing word relationships and grammatical structures in context.

column2: What is the standard NLP Pipeline (in order)?
column3: Data Collection & cleaning → Preprocessing → Feature Engineering → Modeling → Monitoring.

column2: What is segmentation in text preprocessing?
column3: Breaking documents into their constituent sentences.

column2: What is tokenization?
column3: Breaking sentences into individual units called "tokens".

column2: What is the difference between stemming and lemmatization?
column3: Stemming is faster and rule-based (suffix stripping); lemmatization is more accurate, dictionary-based, returns valid words, and is improved by POS tags.

column2: What does POS Tagging do, and how does it differ from NER?
column3: POS Tagging labels grammatical categories (noun, verb, etc.); NER identifies specific entities like people, locations, or organizations.

column2: Why is mapping speech to text not one-to-one?
column3: Speech is non-categorical, continuous pressure waves, while written text consists of categorical units separated by whitespace.

column2: What is Computational Linguistics?
column3: The intersection of linguistics and computer science, developing computational models and algorithms for processing/understanding human language.

column2: What are the two main components of a Conversational AI Pipeline?
column3: (1) Speech AI: ASR/STT + TTS, and (2) NLP: NLU + NLG.

column2: Trace the full Conversational AI flow from user speech to system speech.
column3: Speech → ASR → Text → NLP processing → Text → TTS → Speech output.

column2: How does Computational Linguistics differ from Conversational AI in focus?
column3: Computational Linguistics has a broader theoretical focus on language understanding; Conversational AI is applied, focused on intelligent agents for specific conversational tasks.

column2: How does Conversational AI differ from Generative AI?
column3: Conversational AI focuses on human-like interactions and maintaining context; Generative AI focuses on creating original content based on learned patterns.

column2: What advantage do natively multimodal speech-to-speech models (e.g., GPT-4o) have over text-based pipelines?
column3: They can directly process and generate audio, enabling understanding of emotion, whisper, singing, and handling interruptions.

column2: What does NLTK's word_tokenize output for "Python is Great! I am using NLTK"?
column3: ['Python', 'is', 'Great', '!', 'I', 'am', 'using', 'NLTK']

column2: What does NLTK's sent_tokenize output for "Python is Great! I am using NLTK"?
column3: ['Python is Great!', 'I am using NLTK']

column2: What is a homonym, and why is it problematic for NLP?
column3: Words with the same pronunciation but different meanings (e.g., their/there); problematic for speech-to-text systems.

column2: What are the three types of ambiguity in NLP?
column3: Lexical (word as verb/noun/adj), Semantic (context interpretation), and Syntactic (phrase attachment).

column2: Match the linguistic level to its unit: Phonology
column3: Phonemes (sound systems within a particular language).

column2: Match the linguistic level to its unit: Morphology
column3: Morphemes — the smallest meaningful units (stems, roots, prefixes, suffixes).

column2: Match the linguistic level to its unit: Syntax
column3: Phrases and sentences (rules for sentence structure).

column2: Match the linguistic level to its unit: Pragmatics
column3: Meaning in discourse context (conversational implicatures).

column2: What does text.concordance("word") do in NLTK?
column3: Shows the word in context with surrounding text.

column2: What does text.similar("word") do?
column3: Returns words appearing in similar contexts.

column2: What does FreqDist(text) compute?
column3: A word frequency distribution — count of each word in the text.

column2: Why convert NLTK's stopword list to a set before filtering?
column3: Sets have much more efficient lookup than lists (O(1) vs O(n)), so no loop is needed for membership checks.

column2: For PorterStemmer, what does ps.stem('eating') return?
column3: 'eat' (the gerund 'ing' rule is applied).

column2: For WordNetLemmatizer, what does lem.lemmatize('ate', pos='v') return?
column3: 'eat' (dictionary lookup with verb POS).

column2: Why does the POS tag matter for lemmatization?
column3: The lemmatizer uses POS to choose the correct dictionary entry; without it, accuracy drops (e.g., 'ate' as noun vs verb).

column2: Define Corpus, Vocabulary, Document, and Token.
column3: Corpus (C) = all text data in a dataset; Vocabulary (V) = set of unique words in the corpus; Document (D) = one text record; Token (W) = individual word in the vocabulary.

column2: What is One-Hot Encoding for words?
column3: Each word is a binary vector mapped to the vocabulary, with a single 1 at the word's position and 0s elsewhere.

column2: What size is the One-Hot Encoding matrix for a document?
column3: (P × Q) where P = number of tokens in the document, Q = vocabulary size.

column2: List the disadvantages of One-Hot Encoding.
column3: Feature explosion with large vocabularies; orthogonal vectors give no word relationships; no measure of word importance; sparse, memory-expensive matrices.

column2: What does it mean that One-Hot vectors are "orthogonal"?
column3: They are mathematically independent — the dot product between any two distinct word vectors is 0, so no semantic relationships are captured.

column2: What is a Bag-of-Words (BoW) document representation?
column3: Each document is a frequency vector mapped to the vocabulary; multiple documents form a Document-Term Matrix (DTM).

column2: What is the size of the BoW Document-Term Matrix?
column3: (N × V) where N = total number of documents, V = vocabulary size.

column2: What is the main limitation of Bag-of-Words?
column3: It ignores word order — "children eat cookies" and "cookies eat children" produce identical vectors.

column2: What is an n-gram?
column3: A contiguous sequence of n words; used for next-word prediction.

column2: Give the unigram, bigram, and trigram split of "I am the king".
column3: Unigram: (I)(am)(the)(king); Bigram: (I am)(am the)(the king); Trigram: (I am the)(am the king).

column2: What is the bigram probability formula?
column3: P(wᵢ | wᵢ₋₁) = count(wᵢ₋₁, wᵢ) / count(wᵢ₋₁).

column2: What is the n-gram tradeoff?
column3: Higher n captures more context but requires exponentially larger corpora; the probability of finding repeated n-grams decreases rapidly as n grows.

column2: What does TF-IDF measure?
column3: The relevance of a word to a document relative to other documents in the corpus — words frequent in one document but rare across the corpus get higher scores.

column2: What is the TF formula?
column3: tf(t, d) = (count of t in d) / (number of words in d).

column2: What is the IDF formula?
column3: idf(t) = log(N / (df(t) + 1)) where N = total number of documents and df(t) = number of documents containing t.

column2: Why does IDF reduce the weight of common words?
column3: Words appearing in many documents have IDF close to zero, so their TF-IDF score is low — they are not useful for distinguishing documents.

column2: Compare One-Hot, BoW, TF-IDF, and N-grams: which capture word order?
column3: Only N-grams capture local word order (n-1 context); One-Hot, BoW, and TF-IDF do not capture order.

column2: Compare One-Hot, BoW, TF-IDF, and N-grams: which capture importance?
column3: TF-IDF captures corpus-relative importance; BoW captures only frequency; One-Hot and N-grams capture no importance.

column2: What is a Vector Space Model (VSM)?
column3: A mathematical framework that represents document vectors in high-dimensional space to compare them semantically.

column2: What is a Context-Free Grammar (CFG)?
column3: A set of production rules (LHS → RHS) that define all well-formed sentences in a language.

column2: What are the four formal components of a grammar?
column3: Nonterminal symbols (uppercase, expandable), terminal symbols (lowercase, final), a start symbol (S), and production rules.

column2: What does ε denote in a CFG?
column3: An empty string, typically used for grammars with infinite possible outputs.

column2: What are the three goals of a CFG?
column3: (1) Permit ambiguity by allowing all possible parses, (2) Limit ungrammaticality via agreement (number/tense/gender/person), (3) Ensure meaningfulness by disallowing semantically invalid sentences.

column2: What is the difference between syntactic and semantic parsing?
column3: Syntactic (dependency) parsing reveals structure (head → dependent); semantic parsing converts a sentence to a logical representation (e.g., typed lambda calculus) for meaning and reasoning.

column2: What does Semantic Role Labeling (SRL) detect?
column3: Who did what to whom centered on the main verb; semantic roles + event = a frame. Used for information extraction.

column2: What is Abstract Meaning Representation (AMR)?
column3: A rooted, directed, acyclic graph where nodes = concepts and edges = semantic relations; functional words are omitted; goal is to eliminate syntactic ambiguity and focus on meaning.

column2: What does AMR's :arg0, :top, :arg1 convention encode?
column3: Who (:arg0) did what (:top) to whom (:arg1) — the core frame structure.

column2: What does AMR handle besides semantic roles?
column3: Entity types, coreference, modality, polarity (negation), and Wikification (linking to Wikipedia).

column2: Compare Graph-based and Transition-based AMR parsing.
column3: Graph-based (JAMR): identify concepts first, then relations between them. Transition-based (CAMR): traverse a dependency tree, predict actions at each node/edge to transform it into AMR.

column2: What are spaCy's strengths versus NLTK's?
column3: spaCy is industrial-strength with optimized tokenization, POS, NER, dependency parsing, and word vectors; NLTK is more academic/research-oriented with extensive corpora.

column2: Which library is best for topic modeling (LDA, LSI) and word2vec?
column3: Gensim.

column2: Which library provides pre-trained models like BERT, GPT, T5 via a pipeline interface?
column3: Transformers (Hugging Face) — e.g., from transformers import pipeline.

column2: Which scikit-learn classes are used for text feature extraction?
column3: CountVectorizer (BoW) and TfidfVectorizer (TF-IDF), from sklearn.feature_extraction.text.

column2: Why is regex preferred over LLMs for data preprocessing?
column3: Regex is deterministic, instant, and free; LLMs are slow, expensive, and non-deterministic for pattern matching.

column2: What does the regex /[wW]oodchuck/ match?
column3: Woodchuck or "woodchuck" (disjunction matches one out of several characters).

column2: What does /[^A-Z]/ match?
column3: Any character that is NOT an uppercase letter (caret inside brackets = negation).

column2: What does /^The/ match?
column3: The only at the start of a line (^ is the start-of-line anchor).

column2: What does \b mean in regex?
column3: A word boundary — e.g., /\bthe\b/ matches "the" but not "other" or "theme".

column2: List the three uses of caret (^) in regex.
column3: (1) Match start of line, (2) Negation inside brackets, (3) Literal caret outside brackets.

column2: What does \d match, and what is its bracket equivalent?
column3: A single digit; equivalent to [0-9].

column2: What does \w match, and what is its bracket equivalent?
column3: A word character (letters, digits, underscore); equivalent to [a-zA-Z_0-9].

column2: List the regex quantifiers and their meanings.
column3: X? = zero or one; X* = zero or more; X+ = one or more; X{n} = exactly n; X{n,m} = at least n, at most m.

column2: What does the regex /[A-Za-z]{2}\d{3}/ match? Give an example.
column3: Exactly 2 letters (any case) followed by exactly 3 digits — e.g., "CS229" or "cs231".

column2: Why use raw strings (r"...") in Python regex?
column3: To avoid double-escaping backslashes; in raw strings, backslashes are not treated as escape characters.

column2: Distinguish re.match, re.search, and re.findall.
column3: re.match: match only at the beginning; re.search: first match anywhere; re.findall: list of all non-overlapping matches.

column2: For sent="This book on tennis cost $3.99 at Walmart" with pattern r"\$\d+\.\d\d", what does re.search return?
column3: A match object with span=(25, 30) and match='$3.99'.

column2: In ((A)(B(C))), how many capture groups exist and what is group 0?
column3: 4 capture groups (plus group 0). Group 0 is always the entire match: 'ABC'. Groups 1–4: 'ABC', 'A', 'BC', 'C'.

column2: For pattern r"(\w+) cost (\$\d+\.\d\d)" applied to "tennis cost $3.99", what are group(0), group(1), group(2)?
column3: group(0)='tennis cost $3.99'; group(1)='tennis'; group(2)='$3.99'.

column2: What does the re.I flag do?
column3: Ignore case during matching.

column2: What does the re.M flag do?
column3: Multi-line mode — ^ and $ match at line boundaries within the string.

column2: What does the re.X flag do?
column3: Verbose mode — allows comments and whitespace in the pattern for readability.

column2: Why use finditer() instead of findall() for capturing groups?
column3: findall() returns a list, so .group() cannot be applied (raises AttributeError). finditer() returns match objects with full group access.

column2: What does re.sub(r'5', 'five', text) do?
column3: Replaces all occurrences of '5' in text with 'five'.

column2: What is the goal of text classification?
column3: To automatically assign labels/categories to text — e.g., spam detection, sentiment analysis, topic identification.

column2: In logistic regression for text classification, how is the prediction made?
column3: A weighted sum of features is passed through the sigmoid function to produce a probability of class membership in [0, 1].

column2: Define Supervised, Unsupervised, and Reinforcement learning.
column3: Supervised: training with labeled data (classification, regression). Unsupervised: training with unlabeled data (clustering). Reinforcement: agent takes actions and receives feedback (decision making).

column2: What is a word embedding?
column3: A numeric vector representation of words/documents where similar meanings yield similar representations; each dimension represents a learned feature.

column2: What semantic property do embeddings capture via vector arithmetic?
column3: Analogies — e.g., King − Man + Woman ≈ Queen; relationships like verb tense, country-capital, age-size are preserved.

column2: Classify by semantics: One-hot, BoW, TF-IDF.
column3: No semantics — purely count- or presence-based; no notion of word similarity.

column2: Classify by semantics: Word2Vec, GloVe, FastText.
column3: Static embeddings — one vector per word, regardless of context. FastText additionally handles out-of-vocabulary words via subwords.

column2: Classify by semantics: ELMo, BERT, GPT, T5.
column3: Contextual embeddings — vectors change based on surrounding context. BERT/GPT/T5 are Transformer-based; ELMo/CoVe are not.

column2: What is the Cosine Similarity formula?
column3: cos(A, B) = (A · B) / (||A|| × ||B||) — the cosine of the angle between two vectors.

column2: What does cosine similarity = 1, 0, and −1 mean?
column3: 1 = identical direction; 0 = orthogonal (no similarity); −1 = opposite direction.

column2: For non-negative word-count vectors, what range does cosine similarity take?
column3: [0, 1] — never negative, since the dot product cannot be negative.

column2: What does Euclidean distance measure?
column3: The straight-line (L2-norm) distance between two vectors in n-dimensional space.

column2: What does model.most_similar(positive=['woman','king'], negative=['man'], topn=1) return?
column3: [('queen', ~0.71)] — solving the analogy King − Man + Woman ≈ Queen.

column2: What does model.doesnt_match('breakfast cereal dinner lunch'.split()) return?
column3: 'cereal' — the odd one out (the others are meals).

column2: What is t-SNE used for?
column3: Dimensionality reduction for visualizing high-dimensional embeddings in 2D while preserving similarity (similar words cluster).

column2: Why do practitioners apply PCA before t-SNE?
column3: PCA reduces to ~50 dimensions first to speed up t-SNE and reduce noise; t-SNE then maps to 2D for visualization.

column2: What is Information Retrieval (IR)?
column3: The task of identifying and retrieving information system resources relevant to an information need (e.g., web search engines).

column2: What are the three components of an IR Model?
column3: Document Template (structure), Query Model (user need representation), and Matching Function (compares queries to documents).

column2: State Bayes' Theorem.
column3: P(A|B) = P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B).

column2: In Bayes' theorem, what are P(A|B), P(B|A), P(A), and P(B) called?
column3: P(A|B) = posterior; P(B|A) = likelihood; P(A) = prior; P(B) = evidence (marginal probability of B).

column2: What is the Naive Bayes classification formula for text?
column3: P(C | w₁, …, wₙ) ∝ P(C) × ∏ᵢ P(wᵢ | C); choose the class with the highest score.

column2: Why is Naive Bayes called "naive"?
column3: It assumes all predictors contribute equally and independently to classification — unrealistic for words but produces good practical results.

column2: What problem does Laplace (additive) smoothing solve?
column3: If a word never appears in a class during training, P(word|C) = 0, making the entire product zero. Adding α (typically 1) ensures no probability is zero.

column2: What is the Laplace-smoothed probability formula?
column3: P(w|C) = (count(w, C) + α) / (total words in C + α × |V|), where |V| is vocabulary size.

column2: When is Multinomial NB preferred over Gaussian or Bernoulli NB?
column3: For discrete word counts (frequencies) — ideal for document/text classification. Gaussian = continuous features; Bernoulli = binary present/absent.

column2: Define Precision and Recall.
column3: Precision = TP / (TP + FP) — of predicted positives, how many are correct. Recall = TP / (TP + FN) — of actual positives, how many were found.

column2: What is Sentiment Analysis (Opinion Mining)?
column3: A text classification NLP task that determines whether the emotional tone of digital text is positive, negative, or neutral.

column2: Distinguish Document-, Sentence-, and Aspect-Based sentiment analysis.
column3: Document-level: classifies the entire text; Sentence-level: per-sentence sentiment; Aspect-Based: sentiment for specific aspects/features mentioned (e.g., performance, design).

column2: What is Fine-Grained sentiment analysis?
column3: Categorization into multiple levels on a 0–100 scale (very negative → very positive); 5-star ratings are an example.

column2: What is the rule-based (lexicon) sentiment approach? Pros and cons?
column3: Uses predetermined word lists with sentiment scores; sums scores and compares to thresholds. Pro: straightforward setup. Con: hard to scale; may miss cultural variations.

column2: Why is sarcasm a challenge for sentiment analysis?
column3: Yeah, great. It took three weeks for my order to arrive may be labeled positive based on "great" without contextual understanding of irony.

column2: What does TextBlob's polarity range mean?
column3: [-1, 1] where −1 = highly negative, 1 = highly positive.

column2: What does TextBlob's subjectivity range mean?
column3: [0, 1] where 0 = factual, 1 = personal opinion.

column2: What is VADER best suited for?
column3: Social media text — short sentences, slang, abbreviations. Less accurate for longer structured text.

column2: What VADER compound score thresholds define positive, neutral, and negative?
column3: Positive: compound ≥ 0.5; Neutral: −0.5 < compound < 0.5; Negative: compound ≤ −0.5.

column2: What is POS Tagging?
column3: Assigning grammatical categories (noun, verb, adjective, etc.) to each word in a text.

column2: Give the Penn Treebank tags for noun, verb, adjective, adverb, determiner, preposition.
column3: NN, VB, JJ, RB, DT, IN.

column2: For the sentence "The quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog", what POS tags would NLTK return for "quick", "fox", "jumps"?
column3: 'quick' → JJ (adjective), 'fox' → NN (noun), 'jumps' → VBZ (verb, 3rd person singular present).

column2: List the four main methods of POS tagging.
column3: Rule-Based, Statistical (HMMs, CRFs), Machine Learning (decision trees, SVMs), Deep Learning (RNNs, LSTMs).

column2: What is dependency in dependency grammar?
column3: A binary asymmetric relation between a head and its dependents; the sentence head is typically the tensed verb.

column2: What approach does dependency parsing use, and what languages does it suit?
column3: Bottom-up; suits languages with less morphological inflection (English, Chinese) and less complex syntax.

column2: What is a projective dependency graph?
column3: One where, when words are written linearly, edges can be drawn above without crossing — equivalently, a word and its descendants form a contiguous substring.

column2: What is Named Entity Recognition (NER)?
column3: The task of (1) finding spans of text that are proper names, and (2) tagging the entity type.

column2: List the four common NER entity types and their meanings.
column3: PER (Person), LOC (Location), ORG (Organization), GPE (Geo-Political Entity).

column2: Why is segmentation harder for NER than for POS tagging?
column3: POS tagging assigns one tag per word; NER must find and segment multi-word entity spans.

column2: What do the BIO prefix tags mean?
column3: B = beginning of an entity, I = inside an entity, O = outside any entity.

column2: What are the limitations of IO and the advantages of BIOES?
column3: IO cannot encode consecutive entities of the same type. BIOES adds E (end) and S (single-token entity) for clearer boundary information.

column2: For n entity types, how many total BIO tags are there?
column3: 2n + 1 — one O tag plus B and I tags for each of the n types.

column2: List the standard NER algorithms.
column3: Hidden Markov Models (HMM), Conditional Random Fields (CRF) / MEMM, Neural sequence models (RNNs, Transformers), Fine-tuned LLMs (e.g., BERT).

column2: Distinguish linguistic and pragmatic meaning.
column3: Linguistic = literal content ("It is 8 pm"). Pragmatic = implied intent ("It is time to leave").

column2: Compare sparse (TF-IDF) and dense (Word2Vec) vectors.
column3: Sparse: high-dimensional (vocab size), mostly zeros, count-based, no semantic similarity. Dense: low-dimensional (50–300), all non-zero, learned from context, captures similarity.

column2: What are typical word embedding dimensions?
Compare CBOW and Skip-gram in Word2Vec.
column3: CBOW: input = context words, predict center word; faster training. Skip-gram: input = single target word, predict surrounding words; better for rare words.

column2: What does GloVe combine?
column3: Word2Vec's local context learning with global word-word co-occurrence statistics.

column2: List Predicate Logic, Semantic Network, Conceptual Dependency, and Frame-Based representations of "I have a car".
column3: Predicate Logic: ∃x,y Having(x) ∧ Haver(Speaker, x) ∧ HadThing(y, x) ∧ Car(y). Semantic Network: Having → {Haver→Speaker, HadThing→Car}. Conceptual Dependency: Speaker —POSS-BY→ Car. Frame-Based: Having { Haver: Speaker, HadThing: Car }.

column2: Name a key embedding limitation involving homophones.
column3: Embeddings cannot distinguish homophones such as brake/break or cell/sell — they share a single representation.

column2: What is WordNet?
column3: A semantically-oriented dictionary of English; NLTK's version includes 155,287 words and 117,659 synsets.

column2: What is a synset and what does the format word.pos.nn mean?
column3: A synset is a collection of synonymous lemmas sharing one meaning; e.g., car.n.01 means the noun sense 01 of "car".

column2: What POS letters does WordNet use?
column3: n=noun, v=verb, r=adverb, a=adjective.

column2: Define hypernym and hyponym with an example.
column3: Hypernym: more general (IS-A parent), e.g., 'animal' is a hypernym of 'dog'. Hyponym: more specific (IS-A child), e.g., 'dog' and 'cat' are hyponyms of 'animal'.

column2: Define holonym and meronym with an example.
column3: Holonym: the whole containing a part (HAS-A parent), e.g., 'bird' is a holonym of 'feathers'. Meronym: a part of a whole (HAS-A child), e.g., 'feathers' and 'beak' are meronyms of 'bird'.

column2: What does s1.lowest_common_hypernyms(s2) return?
column3: The most specific shared ancestor of s1 and s2 in the hypernym taxonomy — e.g., for dog and cat: carnivore.

column2: What does path_similarity measure?
column3: A score in [0, 1] based on the shortest path connecting two synsets in the hypernym/hyponym taxonomy; 1 = identical synsets.

column2: What does wup_similarity measure?
column3: Wu-Palmer similarity in [0, 1] based on the depth of synsets and their lowest common hypernym in the taxonomy.

column2: What is the key distinction between classical ML and deep learning for NLP?
column3: Classical ML: feature extraction (BoW, TF-IDF) and modeling (NB, SVM) are separate manual stages. Deep learning: a single neural network performs both jointly via dense embeddings and hidden layers.

column2: What is a neural unit's computation?
column3: Two steps: (1) z = w · x + b (weighted sum), (2) y = f(z) (apply nonlinear activation).

column2: Compare Sigmoid, tanh, and ReLU activations by range and use.
column3: Sigmoid: (0, 1), used in gates. tanh: (−1, 1), zero-centered, preferred over sigmoid in hidden layers. ReLU: [0, ∞), most common, fast, but suffers from "dead neurons".

column2: What is a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN)?
column3: A neural network with a recurrent loop that passes hidden state from one time step to the next, giving it memory over sequential data.

column2: What two key properties define RNNs?
column3: (1) They account for previous inputs via hidden state, (2) They share weights/parameters across all time steps.

column2: Match RNN architecture types to tasks: Many-to-One.
column3: Sentiment classification — sequence in, single label out from final hidden state.

column2: Match RNN architecture types to tasks: One-to-Many.
column3: Music generation, image captioning — single input, sequence of outputs.

column2: Match RNN architecture types to tasks: Many-to-Many (Tx ≠ Ty).
column3: Machine translation — encoder reads input, decoder generates output of different length.

column2: What is the vanishing gradient problem in RNNs?
column3: During backpropagation through time, gradients are multiplied across time steps; for long sequences they shrink exponentially, preventing learning of long-range dependencies.

column2: What is the exploding gradient problem?
column3: When weights > 1, gradients grow exponentially during BPTT, causing unstable training and diverging weights.

column2: How does GRU address vanishing gradients?
column3: By adding an update gate (controls how much previous state to retain) and a reset gate (controls how much to forget when computing the candidate activation).

column2: What activation functions does a GRU use?
column3: tanh for the candidate hidden state (−1 to +1), sigmoid for gate values (0 to 1), and softmax at the output layer.

column2: How many gates does an LSTM have, versus GRU?
column3: LSTM: 3 gates (forget, input, output). GRU: 2 gates (update, reset).

column2: What is the LSTM cell state update equation?
column3: C_t = f_t ⊙ C_{t−1} + i_t ⊙ C̃_t — old memory scaled by forget gate, plus new candidate scaled by input gate.

column2: What does the LSTM forget gate do?
column3: Sigmoid layer that decides what information to discard from the previous cell state C_{t−1}; output near 0 = forget, near 1 = keep.

column2: What does the LSTM input gate do?
column3: A sigmoid layer (i_t) selects which values to update, paired with a tanh layer that creates candidate values C̃_t.

column2: What does the LSTM output gate do?
column3: A sigmoid layer that decides which parts of the cell state to output: H_t = O_t ⊙ tanh(C_t).

column2: Compare GRU and LSTM in cell state and parameters.
column3: GRU: no separate cell state, hidden state only, fewer parameters (faster). LSTM: separate cell state C_t + hidden state H_t, ~4× more parameters (greater capacity).

column2: In the LSTM Keras snippet model.add(LSTM(256, input_shape=(X.shape[1], X.shape[2]))), what does input_shape represent?
column3: (sequence_length, num_features_per_step) — the second and third dims of the input X.

column2: What does Dropout(0.2) do?
column3: Randomly zeros 20% of inputs during training to prevent overfitting and improve generalization.

column2: What does Dense(y.shape[1], activation='softmax') compute?
column3: A fully connected output layer with one unit per class; softmax produces a probability distribution summing to 1 (multi-class classification).

column2: What loss is used with one-hot encoded multi-class labels?
column3: categorical_crossentropy.

column2: What does the Adam optimizer combine?
column3: Adaptive learning rates from AdaGrad with momentum-like behavior of RMSProp.

column2: Why is sigmoid used in LSTM gates?
column3: It outputs values in (0, 1), acting as a switch: 0 = completely forget, 1 = completely keep.

column2: Why is tanh used for LSTM candidate states?
column3: It outputs values in (−1, 1); its second derivative sustains over a longer range, helping mitigate vanishing gradients.

column2: Compare Standard RNN and LSTM internal state.
column3: RNN: single hidden state h. LSTM: hidden state h + separate cell state c that flows across time with minimal modification.

column2: For stacked LSTMs in Keras, when do you set return_sequences=True?
column3: For all LSTM layers except the last when stacking — earlier layers must output a sequence, while the final layer typically outputs a single vector for the dense head.

column2: What is pretraining versus fine-tuning?
column3: Pretraining: training a model from scratch with random weights on a very large corpus; expensive (days, GPU clusters); produces a general-purpose LM. Fine-tuning: continued training on a small task-specific dataset using a pretrained model.

column2: What are the three vectors in the self-attention mechanism?
column3: Query (Q): what this token is looking for. Key (K): what this token offers. Value (V): the actual content to be aggregated.

column2: State the Scaled Dot-Product Attention formula.
column3: Attention(Q, K, V) = softmax((Q Kᵀ) / √d_k) × V.

column2: Why divide by √d_k in scaled dot-product attention?
column3: To prevent dot products from growing too large, which would push softmax into regions with vanishing gradients.

column2: What are the original Transformer hyperparameters from "Attention Is All You Need"?
column3: h = 8 heads, d_model = 512, d_k = d_v = 64, N = 6 encoder/decoder layers, FFN inner dim = 2048.

column2: What sub-layers are in a Transformer encoder block?
column3: (1) Multi-head self-attention, (2) Position-wise feed-forward network. Each wrapped with residual connection + layer normalization.

column2: What sub-layers are in a Transformer decoder block?
column3: (1) Masked multi-head self-attention, (2) Multi-head cross-attention over encoder output, (3) Position-wise feed-forward network. Each with residual + LayerNorm.

column2: What does the FFN compute in a Transformer?
column3: FFN(x) = max(0, x W₁ + b₁) W₂ + b₂ — applied per position; inner layer dimension = 2048.

column2: List the four steps of self-attention.
column3: (1) Encode position information (positional encoding). (2) Extract Q, K, V via three learned linear layers. (3) Compute attention weighting via scaled dot product. (4) Multiply weights by V to extract features.

column2: List the three attention mechanisms in a full Transformer.
column3: (1) Encoder Self-Attention (bidirectional), (2) Decoder Masked Self-Attention (only past positions), (3) Encoder-Decoder Cross-Attention (decoder queries attend to encoder K/V).

column2: Match Transformer model type to task: Encoder-only.
column3: Sentence classification, NER — tasks requiring input understanding. Example: BERT.

column2: Match Transformer model type to task: Decoder-only.
column3: Generative tasks (text generation). Example: GPT.

column2: Match Transformer model type to task: Encoder-Decoder.
column3: Generative tasks requiring input — translation, summarization. Examples: T5, BART.

column2: Why are Transformers parallelizable while RNNs are not?
column3: Transformers process the entire input at once via self-attention; RNNs process one token at a time, with each step depending on the previous hidden state.

column2: What does the four-row table of Bag-of-unigrams, Bag-of-bigrams, RNN, Self-Attention, Transformer say about word order and cross-word context?
column3: Only RNN and Transformer capture word order. Only Self-Attention and Transformer capture cross-word context. Transformer is the only one with both.

column2: How are Q, K, V computed from an input embedding X?
column3: q = X · Wq, k = X · Wk, v = X · Wv — using three jointly-learned weight matrices.

column2: Why use multiple attention heads?
column3: Each head learns different feature groups per token; the model can attend to information from different representation subspaces simultaneously.

column2: What problem does positional encoding solve?
column3: Self-attention is permutation-invariant — it has no inherent notion of word order. Positional encoding injects sequence position info into input embeddings.

column2: What functions are used for positional encoding, and why two?
column3: Sinusoidal functions: sin for even dimensions (2i), cos for odd dimensions (2i+1). Lower dimensions oscillate rapidly; higher dimensions slowly — encoding both fine and coarse position info.

column2: What is the positional encoding formula for even indices?
column3: PE(pos, 2i) = sin(pos / 10000^(2i / d_model)).

column2: What is the limitation of learned positional embeddings (vs sinusoidal)?
column3: The maximum sequence length must be known in advance — the embedding matrix has a fixed size.

column2: What is teacher forcing?
column3: During training, the decoder receives the ground-truth previous tokens rather than its own predictions; loss is computed via cross-entropy at each position.

column2: What architecture does GPT use?
column3: Decoder-only — only the decoder stack from the original Transformer; the encoder-decoder cross-attention layer is removed.

column2: What does each GPT decoder block contain?
column3: Masked Self-Attention → Feed Forward Neural Network. (No cross-attention block, since there is no encoder.)

column2: Compare BERT and GPT: architecture, attention, training objective.
column3: BERT: encoder-only, bidirectional self-attention, MLM + Next Sentence Prediction. GPT: decoder-only, masked (causal) self-attention, autoregressive next-token prediction.

column2: What are BERT and GPT each best at?
column3: BERT: understanding/classification tasks. GPT: text generation/continuation tasks.

column2: What is GPT-3's parameter count, sequence length, and embedding dimension?
column3: 175B parameters, sequence length 2048, embedding dimension 12,288.

column2: What is the GPT-2 token embedding (wte) matrix shape?
column3: (vocab_size × embedding_dim) = (50,257 × embedding_size); embedding size depends on variant (768 / 1024 / 1280 / 1600).

column2: How does GPT generate text autoregressively?
column3: (1) Feed current sequence through all decoder layers. (2) Multiply final-position output by Wᵀ_wte to produce logits over the vocabulary. (3) Sample a token. (4) Append and repeat.

column2: What is the masking process for masked self-attention?
column3: (1) Compute Q × Kᵀ score matrix. (2) Set positions above the diagonal to −∞. (3) Apply softmax row-wise — −∞ becomes 0. (4) Multiply by V.

column2: Compare Self-Attention and Masked Self-Attention.
column3: Self-Attention: token attends to all positions (past + future), used in encoders (BERT). Masked Self-Attention: token attends only to current and past positions, used in decoders (GPT); upper-triangle is set to −∞ before softmax.

column2: What is GPT's training objective and loss function?
column3: Next-token prediction. Loss: L = −Σₜ log P(xₜ | x_{<t}; θ).

column2: List four GPT risks.
column3: Data Privacy (training on user data), IP/Copyright (reproducing protected content), Hallucinations (confident but fabricated outputs), Model Bias (encoding discriminatory views from training data).

column2: What is Question Answering (QA)?
column3: A subfield of NLP focused on building models that automatically answer natural-language questions with precise natural-language answers. Core components: question parser, knowledge base, answer generator.

column2: List the four QA types.
column3: Factoid (single concrete fact), List (list of facts), Definition (conceptual definition), Scenario (narrative or hypothesis).

column2: Compare Extractive QA, Open Generative QA, and Closed Generative QA.
column3: Extractive: external context, extracts a span (BERT-like). Open Generative: external context, generates answer (T5, BART). Closed Generative: no context, generates from parameters (GPT-3).

column2: What are the three components of a QA system, and what do Reader-only and Reader+Retriever correspond to?
column3: Retriever (gets context), Reader (selects span from question + context), Generator (generates free text). Reader alone = Reading Comprehension; Reader + Retriever = Open-Domain QA.

column2: What are the steps of a QA pipeline?
column3: (1) Text preprocessing, (2) Question understanding (NER, dep parsing, POS), (3) Information retrieval, (4) Answer generation, (5) Ranking by relevance/confidence.

column2: What is the default Hugging Face QA model, and what does it return?
column3: distilbert-base-cased-distilled-squad (extractive open QA). Returns: answer text, start position, end position, confidence score.

column2: For qa_model(question="Where is the ball?", context="The chair is near the table, and the ball is behind the chair."), what is the answer?
column3: 'behind the chair' (start=45, end=61, score≈0.811).

column2: What is a logit in QA?
column3: Raw, unnormalized model output scores before softmax; the QA model outputs start logits and end logits per token, converted to probabilities.

column2: How is the answer span selected?
column3: score[start, end] = start_probabilities[start] × end_probabilities[end]; invalid spans (end < start) are masked; the highest-scoring span wins.

column2: What is SQuAD?
column3: Stanford Question Answering Dataset — primary benchmark for extractive QA: 100,000+ Q-A pairs over 500+ Wikipedia articles; every answer is a text span from the passage.

column2: What does SQuAD v2 add over v1.1?
column3: 50,000+ unanswerable questions designed to look similar to answerable ones. Evaluated using Exact Match (EM) and F1.

column2: What truncation strategy preserves the question and truncates only the context?
column3: truncation="only_second" — truncates only the second input (the context).

column2: What is the sliding window strategy for long contexts?
column3: Split the context into overlapping chunks using return_overflowing_tokens=True and a stride parameter; process each chunk independently and select the best-scoring span across all chunks.

column2: NLP is divided into {{c1::Natural Language Understanding (NLU)}}, which structures unstructured text, and {{c2::Natural Language Generation (NLG)}}, which produces language from data.
The standard NLP Pipeline: Data Collection → {{c1::Preprocessing}} → {{c2::Feature Engineering}} → Modeling → Monitoring.
Stemming is {{c1::rule-based}} (faster, less accurate), while Lemmatization is {{c2::dictionary-based}} (slower, more accurate, returns valid words).
A Conversational AI Pipeline has two parts: {{c1::Speech AI}} (ASR + TTS) and {{c2::NLP}} (NLU + NLG).
NLTK's {{c1::word_tokenize}} splits a string into tokens; {{c2::sent_tokenize}} splits it into sentences.
The three types of ambiguity are: {{c1::Lexical}} (word as verb/noun/adj), {{c2::Semantic}} (context interpretation), and {{c3::Syntactic}} (phrase attachment).
The six levels of language are: {{c1::Phonetics}}, {{c2::Phonology}}, {{c3::Morphology}}, {{c4::Syntax}}, {{c5::Semantics}}, {{c6::Pragmatics}}.
The smallest meaningful units of a language are called {{c1::morphemes}}; the smallest sound units within a particular language are {{c2::phonemes}}.
{{c1::FreqDist(text)}} returns a word frequency distribution; {{c2::text.collocations()}} returns frequently co-occurring word pairs.
WordNetLemmatizer accepts POS tags: {{c1::'n'}} (noun), {{c2::'v'}} (verb), {{c3::'a'}} (adjective), {{c4::'r'}} (adverb).
A {{c1::Corpus}} is all text data in a dataset; a {{c2::Vocabulary}} is the set of unique words; a {{c3::Document}} is one text record; a {{c4::Token}} is an individual word.
One-Hot Encoding produces an (P × Q) matrix where P = {{c1::number of tokens in the document}} and Q = {{c2::vocabulary size}}.
The Bag-of-Words Document-Term Matrix has size (N × V) where N = {{c1::total number of documents}} and V = {{c2::vocabulary size}}.
The bigram probability formula: P(wᵢ | wᵢ₋₁) = {{c1::count(wᵢ₋₁, wᵢ) / count(wᵢ₋₁)}}.
Term Frequency: tf(t, d) = {{c1::count of t in d / total words in d}}.
Inverse Document Frequency: idf(t) = {{c1::log(N / (df(t) + 1))}}.
TF-IDF is computed as: tf-idf(t, d) = {{c1::tf(t, d) × idf(t)}}.
Of the four methods (One-Hot, BoW, TF-IDF, N-grams), only {{c1::N-grams}} captures local word order, and only {{c2::TF-IDF}} captures corpus-relative importance.
A CFG has four components: {{c1::nonterminal symbols}} (uppercase), {{c2::terminal symbols}} (lowercase), a {{c3::start symbol}} (S), and {{c4::production rules}}.
In CFG notation, {{c1::ε}} denotes an empty string, used for grammars with infinite possible outputs.
AMR is a {{c1::rooted, directed, acyclic}} graph where nodes are {{c2::concepts}} and edges are {{c3::semantic relations}}.
AMR encodes "who did what to whom" via {{c1:::arg0}} (who), {{c2:::top}} (what), and {{c3:::arg1}} (to whom).
{{c1::Graph-based}} AMR parsing (JAMR) identifies concepts first, then relations; {{c2::Transition-based}} parsing (CAMR) traverses a dependency tree predicting actions.
{{c1::Gensim}} is the Python library specializing in topic modeling (LDA, LSI), document similarity, and word2vec.
In regex, {{c1::^}} matches start of line, {{c2::$}} matches end of line, {{c3::\b}} matches a word boundary.
Regex predefined classes: {{c1::\d}} = a digit ([0-9]), {{c2::\s}} = whitespace, {{c3::\w}} = word character ([a-zA-Z_0-9]).
Regex quantifiers: {{c1::?}} = 0 or 1, {{c2::*}} = 0 or more, {{c3::+}} = 1 or more, {{c4::{n} }} = exactly n times.
{{c1::re.match}} matches only at the beginning of a string; {{c2::re.search}} returns the first match anywhere; {{c3::re.findall}} returns a list of all non-overlapping matches.
In Python regex, {{c1::raw strings (r"...")}} are used to avoid double-escaping backslashes.
Regex flags: {{c1::re.I}} = ignore case, {{c2::re.M}} = multi-line, {{c3::re.S}} = dot matches all (incl. newline), {{c4::re.X}} = verbose.
Use {{c1::finditer()}} (not findall()) when you need to call .group() on each match — findall returns a list of strings, not match objects.
{{c1::re.sub(pattern, repl, text)}} substitutes all occurrences of the pattern in text with repl.
The three ML paradigms: {{c1::Supervised}} (labeled data, classification/regression), {{c2::Unsupervised}} (unlabeled, clustering), {{c3::Reinforcement}} (action-feedback, decision making).
The classic embedding analogy: {{c1::Queen}} ≈ King − Man + Woman; and {{c2::France}} ≈ Germany − Berlin + Paris.
By semantic capability: One-hot/BoW/TF-IDF capture {{c1::no semantics}}; Word2Vec/GloVe/FastText are {{c2::static embeddings}}; ELMo/BERT/GPT/T5 are {{c3::contextual embeddings}}.
Cosine Similarity: cos(A, B) = {{c1::A · B / (||A|| × ||B||)}}; range [{{c2::−1}}, {{c3::1}}] generally.
For non-negative count vectors, cosine similarity has range [{{c1::0}}, {{c2::1}}].
{{c1::CBOW}} predicts the center word from context (faster); {{c2::Skip-gram}} predicts surrounding words from a target word (better for rare words).
{{c1::t-SNE}} is a dimensionality reduction technique that compresses high-dim embeddings to 2D while preserving similarity, often run after {{c2::PCA}} to ~50 dims for speed.
Bayes' Theorem: P(A|B) = {{c1::P(B|A) × P(A) / P(B)}}.
In Bayes' theorem: {{c1::P(A|B)}} = posterior, {{c2::P(B|A)}} = likelihood, {{c3::P(A)}} = prior, {{c4::P(B)}} = evidence.
The Naive Bayes classification rule: P(C | w₁, …, wₙ) ∝ {{c1::P(C) × ∏ᵢ P(wᵢ | C)}}; choose the class with the highest score.
Naive Bayes is "naive" because it assumes predictors contribute {{c1::equally and independently}} to classification.
Laplace smoothing: P(w|C) = {{c1::(count(w, C) + α) / (total words in C + α × |V|)}}, typically α = 1.
Naive Bayes types: {{c1::Gaussian}} for continuous Gaussian features, {{c2::Bernoulli}} for binary features, {{c3::Multinomial}} for word counts (ideal for text).
Precision = {{c1::TP / (TP + FP)}}; Recall = {{c2::TP / (TP + FN)}}.
Sentiment Analysis types: {{c1::Document-Level}} (whole text), {{c2::Sentence-Level}} (per sentence), {{c3::Aspect-Based}} (per feature), {{c4::Entity-Level}} (per entity).
TextBlob's {{c1::polarity}} is in [−1, 1], and {{c2::subjectivity}} is in [0, 1].
VADER thresholds: Positive ≥ {{c1::0.5}}; Negative ≤ {{c2::−0.5}}; Neutral is between these.
{{c1::VADER}} is best suited for social media text; {{c2::TextBlob}} returns polarity and subjectivity.
Penn Treebank POS tags: {{c1::NN}} = noun, {{c2::VB}} = verb, {{c3::JJ}} = adjective, {{c4::RB}} = adverb, {{c5::DT}} = determiner.
The four POS tagging methods are: {{c1::Rule-Based}}, {{c2::Statistical}} (HMMs/CRFs), {{c3::Machine Learning}}, and {{c4::Deep Learning}} (RNNs/LSTMs).
Common NER entity types: {{c1::PER}} (Person), {{c2::LOC}} (Location), {{c3::ORG}} (Organization), {{c4::GPE}} (Geo-Political Entity).
NER prefix tags in BIOES: {{c1::B}} = beginning, {{c2::I}} = inside, {{c3::O}} = outside, {{c4::E}} = end, {{c5::S}} = single token.
For n entity types, the BIO scheme uses {{c1::2n + 1}} total tags.
The {{c1::IO}} scheme cannot encode consecutive entities of the same type; the {{c2::BIO}} scheme is the standard choice.
Standard NER algorithms: {{c1::HMMs}}, {{c2::CRFs}} / MEMMs, neural sequence models (RNNs, Transformers), and fine-tuned LLMs like BERT.
WordNet relations: {{c1::Hypernym}} = more general (animal → dog); {{c2::Hyponym}} = more specific; {{c3::Holonym}} = whole containing part (bird → feathers); {{c4::Meronym}} = part of a whole.
Synset format: {{c1::word.pos.nn}} (e.g., car.n.01); POS letters: n=noun, v=verb, {{c2::r}}=adverb, {{c3::a}}=adjective.
{{c1::path_similarity}} uses shortest-path distance in the taxonomy; {{c2::wup_similarity}} (Wu-Palmer) uses synset depth and the lowest common hypernym.
Sigmoid range: ({{c1::0, 1}}); tanh range: ({{c2::−1, 1}}); ReLU range: [{{c3::0, ∞}}).
GRU has {{c1::2}} gates ({{c2::update, reset}}); LSTM has {{c3::3}} gates ({{c4::forget, input, output}}).
LSTM cell state update: C_t = {{c1::f_t ⊙ C_{t−1} + i_t ⊙ C̃_t}}.
LSTM hidden state: H_t = {{c1::O_t ⊙ tanh(C_t)}}.
LSTMs use {{c1::sigmoid}} for gates (0–1 switch behavior) and {{c2::tanh}} for candidate states (mitigates vanishing gradient).
RNNs suffer from {{c1::vanishing gradients}} when weights < 1, and {{c2::exploding gradients}} when weights > 1, due to BPTT multiplication across time steps.
LSTM has ~{{c1::4×}} more parameters than a standard RNN due to four gate layers.
Self-attention's three vectors: {{c1::Query}} (what I'm looking for), {{c2::Key}} (what I offer), {{c3::Value}} (the actual content).
Scaled Dot-Product Attention: Attention(Q, K, V) = {{c1::softmax(Q Kᵀ / √d_k) × V}}.
Division by {{c1::√d_k}} prevents dot products from growing too large, which would push softmax into low-gradient regions.
Original Transformer hyperparameters: h = {{c1::8}} heads, d_model = {{c2::512}}, d_k = d_v = {{c3::64}}, N = {{c4::6}} stacked layers.
Encoder block sub-layers: {{c1::multi-head self-attention}} → Add & Norm → {{c2::feed-forward network}} → Add & Norm.
Decoder block sub-layers: {{c1::masked self-attention}} → Add & Norm → {{c2::encoder-decoder cross-attention}} → Add & Norm → feed-forward → Add & Norm.
Q = {{c1::X · Wq}}, K = {{c2::X · Wk}}, V = {{c3::X · Wv}} — all weight matrices are jointly learned during training.
The four steps of self-attention: (1) {{c1::Encode position}}, (2) {{c2::Extract Q, K, V}}, (3) {{c3::Compute attention weighting via scaled dot product}}, (4) {{c4::Multiply weights by V}}.
The three attention mechanisms in a Transformer: {{c1::Encoder Self-Attention}}, {{c2::Decoder Masked Self-Attention}}, {{c3::Encoder-Decoder Cross-Attention}}.
Positional encoding uses {{c1::sin}} for even indices (2i) and {{c2::cos}} for odd indices (2i+1), with denominator {{c3::10000^(2i / d_model)}}.
{{c1::BERT}} is encoder-only with bidirectional attention; {{c2::GPT}} is decoder-only with masked (causal) attention; {{c3::T5/BART}} is encoder-decoder.
GPT is trained with {{c1::next-token prediction}} (autoregressive); BERT is trained with {{c2::Masked Language Modeling (MLM)}} + Next Sentence Prediction.
GPT-3: {{c1::175B}} parameters, sequence length {{c2::2048}}, embedding dim {{c3::12,288}}, {{c4::96}} attention heads, {{c5::96}} decoder layers.
GPT-2 token embedding (wte) shape: (vocab_size × embedding_dim) = ({{c1::50,257}} × embedding_size).
Masked self-attention sets positions {{c1::above the diagonal}} to {{c2::−∞}} before softmax, so they become 0 after normalization.
GPT loss function: L = {{c1::−Σₜ log P(xₜ | x_{<t}; θ)}}.
The four GPT risks: {{c1::Data Privacy}}, {{c2::IP/Copyright}}, {{c3::Hallucinations}}, {{c4::Model Bias}}.
The four QA types: {{c1::Factoid}}, {{c2::List}}, {{c3::Definition}}, {{c4::Scenario}}.

QA components: {{c1::Retriever}} (gets context), {{c2::Reader}} (selects span), {{c3::Generator}} (generates free text). Reader alone = {{c4::Reading Comprehension}}; Reader + Retriever = {{c5::Open-Domain QA}}.
Extractive QA uses {{c1::BERT-like}} models; Open Generative QA uses {{c2::T5/BART}}; Closed Generative QA uses {{c3::GPT-3}}.
{{c1::SQuAD}} is the primary benchmark for extractive QA: {{c2::100,000+}} Q-A pairs over {{c3::500+}} Wikipedia articles.
SQuAD v2 adds {{c1::50,000+ unanswerable questions}}; evaluation metrics are {{c2::Exact Match (EM)}} and {{c3::F1}}.
For long contexts in HF QA, use {{c1::truncation="only_second"}} to keep the question intact, and a {{c2::sliding window with stride}} (return_overflowing_tokens=True) to process overlapping chunks.
QA answer span score: score[start, end] = {{c1::start_probabilities[start] × end_probabilities[end]}}; invalid spans where end < start are masked.
